code_blocks.code:
dataset = xdr_data
| filter _time > to_timestamp(current_time() - 86400 * 14, "SECONDS")
| filter event_type = ENUM.PROCESS and event_sub_type = ENUM.PROCESS_START
| filter lowercase(action_process_image_name) = "sc.exe"
| filter lowercase(action_process_image_command_line) contains " create "
| filter lowercase(action_process_image_command_line) contains "binpath="
    and (lowercase(action_process_image_command_line) contains "powershell"
        or lowercase(action_process_image_command_line) contains "cmd.exe"
        or lowercase(action_process_image_command_line) contains "wscript"
        or lowercase(action_process_image_command_line) contains "cscript"
        or lowercase(action_process_image_command_line) contains "rundll32")
| fields _time, agent_hostname, actor_effective_username,
    causality_actor_process_image_name, action_process_image_command_line
| limit 200

scenario: Service created with sc.exe pointing at a script interpreter.
snippet:
Scenario: imagine an operator persisting (or moving laterally) by creating
a Windows service whose binary path is a script interpreter rather than a
proper executable. The `binPath=` argument tells the story.

- https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1543/003/
- https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-server/administration/windows-commands/sc-create

Logic: legitimate vendor installers create services pointing at their own
signed binaries; an interpreter in `binPath=` is a strong indicator of
operator-driven persistence.
